summary: Dan Shapiro recounts crashing a 500 Startups demo day after a chance encounter with a former cofounder on a flight to San Jose. He provides brief reviews of standout companies from the batch of 32 startups, spanning consumer apps, content personalization, fitness gamification, and children's media. The post captures the energy and eclecticism of early-stage startup culture circa 2012, with Shapiro offering candid takes on each company's strengths and weaknesses. He highlights the global reach of 500 Startups, noting companies from Japan to Brazil in the portfolio.
key_points: 500 Startups had $29MM under management with over 250 investments, half outside Silicon Valley, showcasing early global accelerator reach
Shapiro evaluates startups with a practical lens—praising TinyReview's fun factor while questioning its viability as an actual review platform
Several companies exemplified 2012-era trends: content personalization (Spinnakr), gamification (Fitocracy), and user-generated video (Switchcam)
The demo day snapshot captures a pre-AI startup ecosystem focused on social, mobile, and consumer internet plays
money_quotes: As with all good things, it came about via a stupid coincidence.
You go somewhere, take a picture, and put 3 extremely short lines of text on it – two or three words per line, tops.
It looks like a great creative palette but not like a great reviews site.
Not so in love with knowingly hosting large quantities of pirated content, and automatically categorizing it for easy takedown notices.
They're doing online and mobile storybooks for kids – great growth, and spectacular stickiness with an average session length of 29 minutes.
GoVoluntr has the distinction of being the only startup pitched by an actual Got Milk ad model.
key_insight: A strong demo day showcases not just ideas but founder stories and creative positioning, with the best pitches combining clear market insight with memorable presentation.
